description: Summary of CNBC jobless claim articles on IJC announcement days.The data collection process is described in Section 2.1. Top plot: number of articles we are able to scrape from the CNBC website each year. Bottom plot: in a rolling 60-week window (time stamp = last day of the rolling window), the fraction of articles with bad (good) IJC surprises is shown in the top blue (bottom red) area. The last 60-week rolling window is from 20200130 to 20210318 (end of the paper sample). Other details can be found in Online Appendix OB. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)https://www.cnbc.com/jobless-claims/.

What do people talk about on IJC announcement days?This figure shows the topic mentions in rolling 60-week windows of news articles released on IJC announcement days. The four topic mentions are scaled by mentions of normal IJC words (see Section 2.1 for more details). For instance, 0.2 in the y-axis means that this topic’s keywords are mentioned 20 times per 100 normal IJC words. The timestamp refers to the last day of the rolling window.

What do people talk about on bad and good IJC announcement days?This figure complements Fig. 2 and shows the relative topic mentions on bad (thick lines) and good (thin lines) IJC days within the same 60-week rolling window. For interpretation purposes, each line is scaled with the first value in its series. For instance, 1.5 in the y-axis means that mentions of this topic are 50% higher than at the beginning of the sample. The timestamp always refers to the last day of the rolling window.
Relation between stock variable responses and IJC shocks from February 2020 to March 2021.This figure demonstrates the data points that produce Table 2.

Cross-sectional evidence: Industry keyword mentions in the CARES Act and industry returns-IJC correlations.This figure depicts the relationship between industry returns-IJC shock correlations and their mentions in the actual final Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security (CARES) Act. Construction of industry-level correlation (y-axis): we calculate the correlations between individual stock returns and the IJC shocks of the S&P 500 universe from February 2020 to March 2021. We drop shock outliers and major macro and monetary policy announcement dates. We use 2-digit NAICS codes to classify firms into industries and then calculate the industry-level average correlation. Six industries have fewer than 5 firms representing them among the S&P 500 stocks, and they are therefore excluded from this analysis. Construction of industry mentions in the actual bill (x-axis): We generate industry-level keyword lists from the 6-digit NAICS industry classification webpages. For instance, keywords for “21 Mining” are obtained from https://www.naics.com/six-digit-naics/?v=2017&code=21. Then, we identify mentions of this industry in the actual bills. The CARES Act: This bill was initially introduced in the House of Representatives on January 24, 2019 as H.R. 748, the Middle Class Health Benefits Tax Repeal Act of 2019; it passed the House on July 17, 2019. It then passed the Senate as the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act on March 25, 2020, and was signed into law by President Donald Trump on March 27, 2020. In Fig. A.2, we reproduce the same plot using the HEROES, CAA, and ARP Acts as robustness tests. The fitted line above has a significant and high correlation of 0.44 (SE = 0.24).

Cross-sectional evidence: Obligated Paycheck Protection Program awards and return-IJC correlations.This figure summarizes the relationship between individual stock returns-IJC correlations (y-axis) and the log of total obligated PPP awards (x-axis) from February 2020 to March 2021. For demonstration purposes, we sort firms into four groups by their obligated PPP awards: (i) Not a Covid funding recipient (log(award+1) = 0); (ii) log(award+1) from 0 to 10; (iii) log(award+1) from 10 to 15; (iv) log(award+1) above 15. The dashed lines indicate the actual 90% confidence interval.

Total amount of fiscal spending to firms during the paper sample period.This figure plots total fiscal spending amounts using a 12-month rolling window. The thick solid line represents procurement obligations plus Covid-related stimulus spending; the thin dashed line represents procurement only. Both variables are in billions of dollars, and are based on the authors’ own construction and raw data from USAspending.gov. We systematically limit our firm universe to S&P 500 firms (as we focus on these in the paper). Appendix Figure OA1 shows more details with industry decomposition of the procurement line.

Public attention paid to fiscal stimulus versus government procurement, according to Google Search.This figure depicts the relative public attention paid to two different fiscal spending tools at the monthly frequency from 01/2013 to 04/2021 (one month after the end of our paper sample for completeness), with the first data point under the Procurement line (green) scaled to 1. For stimulus, we use two simple terms: economic stimulus and Covid stimulus; for procurement, we use two other simple terms: procurement and government contracts. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Traditional firm characteristics.We sort S&P500 firms into 5 bins based on firms’ end-of −2019 characteristics: (1) standard size and value factor (B/M, E/P); (2) free cash flow (FCF = operating cash flow (OANCF)-gross capital expenditures (CAPX)); (3) risk (leverage = (long-term debt+short-term debt)/shareholder equity). The portfolio takes the return difference between the lowest (lowest-size, lowest-B/M, lowest-E/P, lowest-FCF, lowest-leverage) and the highest quintile bins. In each portfolio, average returns can be calculated using bad IJC days (when the actual IJC number is higher/worse than expected), good IJC days (when the actual IJC number is lower/better than expected), and non-IJC days. Returns are in basis points; our sample period runs from February 2020 to March 2021, excluding 03/19, 03/26, and 04/02 of 2020 and other macro and FOMC overlaps. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
